subtopic_detail: Immediate needle decompression for tension pneumothorax in trauma
stem: You are the emergency department registrar at a level 1 trauma centre receiving a 32-year-old man brought in by ambulance after a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 minutes ago. He sustained blunt right chest trauma against the steering wheel. He is in severe respiratory distress, agitated and diaphoretic. Trachea is deviated to the left, breath sounds are absent on the right with hyper-resonance to percussion, JVP is markedly elevated, and he is profoundly hypotensive despite a 500 mL crystalloid bolus en route. No chest X-ray has yet been performed and the resuscitation team is in attendance.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4, HR 132, BP 78/42, RR 38, SpO2 86% on 15 L non-rebreather mask, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Tracheal deviation to left, absent right breath sounds, hyper-resonance to percussion right hemithorax, JVP raised, distended neck veins, no pelvic instability, no obvious external haemorrhage
data_table.Investigations: No CXR or CT yet performed, bedside ultrasound shows absent right lung sliding with lung point at mid-axillary line, FAST otherwise negative, no obvious pericardial effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Urgent CT chest with intravenous contrast to confirm the diagnosis of tension pneumothorax and characterise other associated thoracic injuries such as haemothorax, lung contusion, or great vessel injury before any procedural intervention
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis and imaging delays in this presentation are life-threatening; immediate decompression is required. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition: Chest Trauma)

options.text: Continue high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask and observe for response over the next 5 to 10 minutes, with reassessment of breath sounds and saturations before any invasive procedure is considered to avoid iatrogenic harm
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is unsafe in tension pneumothorax; cardiovascular collapse and arrest can occur within minutes. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition: Chest Trauma)

options.text: Perform rapid sequence intubation with positive pressure ventilation now using ketamine and rocuronium, before chest decompression, in order to secure the airway given the GCS 14 and ongoing respiratory failure with progressive desaturation
options.rationale: Positive pressure ventilation worsens tension pneumothorax; decompress the chest first before intubation if possible. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition: Chest Trauma)

options.text: Immediate needle decompression with a large-bore cannula at the 4th-5th intercostal space anterior axillary line on the right (or 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line if 5th IC anatomy precludes), followed by definitive intercostal catheter insertion
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical emergency requiring immediate needle decompression then intercostal catheter; ATLS now favours the 4th-5th IC anterior axillary line for greater chest-wall penetration in adults. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition: Chest Trauma)

options.text: Emergency pericardiocentesis via subxiphoid approach with bedside ultrasound guidance as the haemodynamic compromise with raised JVP is most likely due to cardiac tamponade from blunt cardiac injury rather than tension pneumothorax
options.rationale: Examination findings (tracheal deviation, hyper-resonance, absent right breath sounds, ultrasound absent lung sliding) confirm tension pneumothorax rather than tamponade. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition: Chest Trauma)

explanation.summary: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis based on respiratory distress, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, absent breath sounds and hyper-resonance, with progressive haemodynamic compromise from impaired venous return. Immediate needle decompression with a large-bore cannula is the life-saving intervention. ATLS 10th edition favours the 4th-5th intercostal space anterior axillary line in adults because chest wall thickness in obese patients often exceeds standard 14-gauge cannula length at the 2nd IC mid-clavicular line. Definitive intercostal catheter follows.
key_points: Clinical diagnosis; do not delay for imaging
Needle decompression: 4th-5th IC anterior axillary line (or 2nd IC mid-clavicular)
Definitive: intercostal catheter (size 28-32 Fr) in safe triangle
explanation.pearls: Positive pressure ventilation worsens tension physiology; decompress before intubation where possible. Bedside ultrasound (absent lung sliding, lung point) has higher sensitivity than CXR for pneumothorax in trauma.

sources.label: Australasian College for Emergency Medicine: Trauma resuscitation

subtopic_detail: Lorazepam challenge for catatonia in a patient on aripiprazole
stem: You are the consultation liaison psychiatry registrar asked to review a 24-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia on aripiprazole 30 mg daily, brought in by her family with one week of progressive mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility on examination, echolalia, and refusal of food and fluids. Examination shows BFCRS score 14 with prominent immobility, mutism, posturing, staring and grimacing. She is afebrile and haemodynamically stable. CK is 1200 with normal renal function and otherwise normal investigations including TSH, calcium, and a non-contrast CT brain.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, BFCRS 14
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, posturing with waxy flexibility, echolalia, staring, grimacing, no fever, no autonomic instability, no muscular rigidity beyond posturing
data_table.Investigations: CK 1200 (RR <200), eGFR 95, TSH 1.8, corrected calcium 2.38, FBC and UEC normal, urine drug screen negative, CT brain unremarkable, BFCRS 14

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Increase the aripiprazole dose to 30 mg daily intramuscularly with concurrent prophylactic benztropine 2 mg IM to reduce extrapyramidal side effects and target the underlying psychotic illness that is presumably driving the catatonic state
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and risk progression to malignant catatonia or NMS; they should be reduced or paused while catatonia is treated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic: Catatonia)

options.text: Administer lorazepam 1-2 mg IV (or IM) as a challenge test, observe for clinical response over 30 minutes (improvement in mutism, immobility, posturing), commence regular lorazepam 6-8 mg/day if response is positive, and escalate or proceed to ECT if no response
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge is the first-line test and treatment for catatonia; a partial response within 30 minutes guides ongoing scheduled lorazepam dosing, with ECT for refractory or malignant catatonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic: Catatonia)

options.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg IM as initial treatment for the underlying psychotic symptoms with planned regular dosing every 6 hours, monitoring for extrapyramidal side effects and dystonia in the first 24 hours of therapy
options.rationale: Haloperidol can precipitate malignant catatonia or NMS in a catatonic patient; antipsychotics should not be used to treat catatonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic: Catatonia)

options.text: Commence clozapine 12.5 mg with planned titration over 2 weeks given the failure of aripiprazole to control her psychotic illness, with weekly FBC monitoring per the clozapine protocol and adjunctive lorazepam if needed
options.rationale: Clozapine is used in treatment-resistant schizophrenia after multiple trials but is not the first-line agent for catatonia; lorazepam challenge precedes any antipsychotic change. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic: Catatonia)

options.text: Proceed directly to electroconvulsive therapy after Mental Health Tribunal authorisation as the most rapidly effective therapy for catatonia, without trial of lorazepam first, given the severity of her clinical state and the urgency of treatment
options.rationale: ECT is the next step after lorazepam challenge failure (or for malignant catatonia or rapid deterioration); lorazepam trial precedes ECT in non-malignant catatonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic: Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is recognised by motor (immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility), behavioural (mutism, negativism, echolalia, echopraxia) and autonomic features. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (BFCRS) supports recognition. First-line treatment is a lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV or IM), with positive response within 30 minutes guiding scheduled lorazepam at 6-8 mg/day (sometimes higher). Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and risk progression to malignant catatonia or NMS. ECT is the definitive treatment in lorazepam non-responders and in malignant catatonia.
key_points: First-line: lorazepam 1-2 mg IV/IM challenge then 6-8 mg/day scheduled
Avoid antipsychotics in catatonia (risk of malignant catatonia/NMS)
ECT for refractory or malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (catatonia plus fever and autonomic instability) overlaps clinically with NMS and serotonin syndrome; immediate cessation of antipsychotics, supportive care, lorazepam and urgent ECT are required.

subtopic_detail: Mixed cardiogenic and septic shock with line-related bacteraemia post-STEMI
stem: You are the ICU registrar reviewing a 72-year-old woman day 3 post-large anterior STEMI treated with primary PCI to the LAD. She is now hypotensive on noradrenaline 0.4 mcg/kg/min, with a new fever (38.9) since this morning. WCC is 19, lactate 4.8 (up from 2.1 on day 2). Bedside echo shows severe LV systolic dysfunction (EF 25 percent), no tamponade, no new regional wall motion abnormality. CVP 18, ScvO2 55 percent, cardiac index by PiCCO 1.6 L/min/m squared, urine output 10 mL/hr over the last 2 hours. Blood cultures from the femoral central line (in situ 4 days) are growing a gram-negative bacillus identified provisionally as E. coli.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9, HR 124 sinus tachycardia, BP 78/45 on noradrenaline 0.4 mcg/kg/min, RR 26, SpO2 95% on FiO2 0.5
data_table.Examination: Cool peripheries, mottled knees, raised JVP, bibasal crackles, S3 gallop, femoral central line in situ day 4 with mild erythema at site
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.8, ScvO2 55%, CVP 18, CI 1.6 L/min/m sq, EF 25% on echo, no tamponade, blood cultures growing GNB from femoral line and peripheral; urine output 10 mL/hr

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next haemodynamic intervention?

options.text: Increase noradrenaline to 1.0 mcg/kg/min and give a 1 litre Hartmann bolus over 30 minutes to support BP and intravascular volume, with continued current antibiotic regimen and review of inotrope after the fluid bolus
options.rationale: High-dose noradrenaline with low CI 1.6 will worsen afterload and tissue perfusion; a 1 L bolus with CVP 18 and pulmonary congestion risks worsening pulmonary oedema. (Source: ANZICS Septic Shock Guidance; ESC Cardiogenic Shock Position Paper)

options.text: Switch noradrenaline to vasopressin 0.04 units/min as monotherapy and add hydrocortisone 50 mg every 6 hours, without addressing inotropy or source control, while continuing the current empirical antibiotic regimen unchanged
options.rationale: Vasopressin and steroid are appropriate adjuncts in septic shock but do not address the cardiogenic component (low CI, low ScvO2) or the infected line; source control and inotropy are missing. (Source: ANZICS Septic Shock Guidance)

options.text: Diurese aggressively with frusemide 80 mg IV bolus and ongoing infusion targeting negative balance of 1 to 2 litres in 24 hours given the high CVP 18 and bibasal crackles representing pulmonary oedema and fluid overload
options.rationale: Diuresis in distributive plus cardiogenic shock with poor cardiac output worsens organ perfusion; inotropic support and source control take priority. (Source: ESC Cardiogenic Shock Position Paper)

options.text: Add an inotrope (dobutamine 2.5-5 mcg/kg/min targeting CI >2.2), broaden antibiotics to piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin pending sensitivities, remove and replace the infected femoral line, and refer to cardiothoracics for IABP or Impella consideration
options.rationale: Mixed cardiogenic plus septic shock requires inotropic support for the low cardiac output, broader empirical antibiotic cover, source control by removing the infected line, and mechanical circulatory support consideration in refractory cardiogenic shock. (Source: ANZICS Septic Shock Guidance; ESC Cardiogenic Shock Position Paper)

options.text: Defer all changes pending insertion of a pulmonary artery catheter to fully characterise the haemodynamic profile before commencing or modifying any therapy, given the diagnostic uncertainty between predominantly cardiogenic and septic components
options.rationale: PiCCO and ScvO2 already characterise the mixed shock; delaying therapy for PA catheter is not the right priority in this time-critical scenario. (Source: ESC Cardiogenic Shock Position Paper)

explanation.summary: Mixed cardiogenic and septic shock after large anterior STEMI with line-related bacteraemia requires simultaneous management of both components: inotropic support (dobutamine, or milrinone if pulmonary hypertension) for the cardiogenic component evidenced by low CI and low ScvO2; broader empirical antibiotics pending sensitivities for the septic component; source control by removal and replacement of the infected central line; and consideration of mechanical circulatory support (IABP, Impella, or VA-ECMO) for refractory cardiogenic shock. Vasopressors alone or fluid alone worsen outcomes in this profile.
key_points: Mixed shock: support BP (vasopressor) + flow (inotrope) + source control
Source control as urgent as antibiotics (remove infected line)
Mechanical circulatory support considered for refractory cardiogenic shock
explanation.pearls: ScvO2 <60% with low CI in a hypotensive patient indicates inadequate cardiac output and tissue oxygen delivery; lactate clearance is the bedside marker of effective resuscitation.

subtopic_detail: Hand foot and mouth disease versus herpangina and HSV stomatitis
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 3-year-old previously well boy with 2 days of fever to 38.5 and refusal to eat due to mouth pain. Examination demonstrates multiple small grey-white vesicles on the palms of both hands (approximately 8 lesions), soles of both feet, and shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and tongue, with no rash on the trunk and no truncal vesicles. His 5-year-old sister had a similar mild illness 1 week ago that resolved spontaneously. He is well-hydrated, alert, interactive with his parents, and there is no neurological deficit, no neck stiffness, no rash on the buttocks or perineum, and immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Small grey-white vesicles on palms and soles (about 8 on hands), shallow oral ulcers on buccal mucosa and tongue, no truncal rash, mucous membranes moist, no lymphadenopathy, no neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: No bloods taken; bedside glucose 5.4; no swabs sent; family confirms no recent travel; immunisations including MMR up to date

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and management?

options.text: Primary herpes simplex stomatitis with widespread painful vesicles and ulcers in the mouth, with oral aciclovir 80 mg/kg/day in divided doses for 5-7 days, paracetamol-based analgesia, and exclusion from childcare until oral lesions have crusted
options.rationale: Primary HSV stomatitis typically causes larger painful gingivostomatitis with peri-oral involvement; absence of peripheral hand and foot vesicles makes HSV unlikely here. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease

options.text: Varicella zoster virus infection (chickenpox) with vesicular eruption progressing from face and trunk centrifugally, with supportive care, anti-pruritic management, and exclusion from childcare for at least 5 days after the rash has appeared
options.rationale: Varicella has a typical centripetal vesicular truncal rash with lesions at multiple stages and posterior pharynx ulcers; peripheral hand and foot distribution with sparing of the trunk is not consistent with varicella. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease

options.text: Hand foot and mouth disease caused by coxsackievirus A16 or enterovirus 71 with supportive care, adequate analgesia, oral fluid encouragement, and exclusion from childcare until vesicles have crusted (usually 7-10 days), with no antiviral therapy required
options.rationale: Hand foot and mouth disease is a clinical diagnosis based on oral ulcers plus peripheral hand and foot vesicles in a well child; management is supportive with childcare exclusion until vesicles crust. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease

options.text: Herpangina from coxsackievirus group A presenting with isolated posterior pharyngeal vesicles and ulcers with high fever, with supportive analgesia and oral hydration, antiviral therapy is not warranted, and exclusion from school until resolution
options.rationale: Herpangina is limited to the soft palate, uvula and tonsillar pillars without peripheral vesicles; this patient has peripheral hand and foot vesicles consistent with hand foot and mouth disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with mucocutaneous involvement requiring urgent admission, cessation of any culprit medication, fluid and electrolyte management, ophthalmology and dermatology input, and dermatology-directed biopsy of representative skin lesions
options.rationale: Stevens-Johnson syndrome presents with multiple mucosal involvement plus target lesions and skin denudation in a systemically unwell child, none of which are present here; this child is well. (Source: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease

explanation.summary: Hand foot and mouth disease (HFMD) is caused most commonly by coxsackievirus A16 or enterovirus 71 (EV71 implicated in more severe and rare neurological complications). Clinical features are fever, painful oral ulcers (anywhere in the mouth, often anterior), and a vesicular rash on hands, feet and sometimes buttocks. Management is supportive with adequate analgesia and oral fluid encouragement. Children should be excluded from childcare until vesicles have crusted (usually 7-10 days). Adequate hand hygiene reduces household transmission.
key_points: Triad: fever + oral ulcers + peripheral vesicles (hands and feet)
Coxsackievirus A16 most common; EV71 occasionally causes severe disease
Supportive care only; exclude from childcare until vesicles crust
explanation.pearls: Watch for atypical EV71 disease (myocarditis, brainstem encephalitis, neurogenic pulmonary oedema) - red flags include high persistent fever, hyperhydrosis, myoclonus and listlessness.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Hand Foot and Mouth Disease

subtopic_detail: Direct colposcopy referral for HPV 16 positive on routine cervical screening
stem: You are the GP reviewing the results of a 30-year-old woman's first cervical screening test (CST) performed 4 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic with no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding, no abnormal discharge, and a macroscopically normal cervix on speculum at the time of collection. Her result shows HPV detected, type 16 positive, and reflex liquid-based cytology (LBC) reports negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy (NILM). Her last sexual partner change was 6 months ago, she has been with her current partner for 4 months, and her vaccination history includes HPV vaccination at age 13 (Gardasil 4-valent, completed). She has no comorbidities and is on no medications.

data_table.Examination: Cervix macroscopically normal at the time of CST collection, no contact bleeding, no visible lesion or polyp, no adnexal mass on bimanual examination
data_table.Investigations: CST: HPV detected, type 16 positive; reflex LBC negative for IEL or malignancy; urine bHCG negative; no other recent investigations available

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 12 months to assess HPV persistence and reflex cytology, with continued routine screening pathway if HPV has cleared spontaneously between now and the recommended follow-up interval
options.rationale: Repeat in 12 months is the pathway for HPV-other-than-16/18 positive with negative LBC, not for HPV 16/18 positive which mandates colposcopy regardless of LBC. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 5 years as part of the routine screening interval, with normal further screening if no new symptoms develop in the interim and continuation of current sexual health and contraceptive practices
options.rationale: Five-yearly screening applies only to HPV-negative results; an HPV 16 positive test requires direct colposcopy referral. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: Commence topical imiquimod 5 percent cream three times per week for 12 weeks for HPV clearance, with cervical screening repeated after completion of treatment to assess for clearance of the underlying HPV 16 infection
options.rationale: Imiquimod has no established role in cervical HPV management; topical immunomodulators are used for external anogenital warts not endocervical HPV. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to a gynaecology colposcopy clinic for colposcopic assessment now, since HPV 16 or 18 detection on cervical screening warrants colposcopy regardless of reflex cytology result due to the higher risk of high-grade dysplasia
options.rationale: Under the Australian NCSP, HPV 16 or 18 positive on CST mandates direct referral to colposcopy regardless of reflex LBC result, because of the higher associated risk of CIN3 or invasive cancer. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: Perform an endometrial pipelle biopsy in the GP rooms to exclude endometrial pathology associated with the abnormal cervical screening test before referral for further specialist gynaecology assessment and ongoing management plan
options.rationale: Endometrial sampling is irrelevant to cervical HPV; it is indicated for abnormal uterine bleeding or post-menopausal bleeding, neither of which is present here. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The Australian National Cervical Screening Program (NCSP) tests primarily for HPV with reflex LBC if HPV is detected. HPV 16 or 18 positive results carry a higher risk of CIN3 or invasive cancer and warrant direct colposcopy referral regardless of LBC result. HPV-other-positive with negative LBC repeats in 12 months. HPV-other-positive with possible or definite HSIL on LBC requires colposcopy. HPV negative results return to 5-yearly routine screening.
key_points: HPV 16/18 positive: colposcopy referral regardless of LBC
HPV-other + negative LBC: repeat in 12 months
explanation.pearls: Self-collection of vaginal HPV swab is now offered to all eligible women in Australia and has equivalent sensitivity for high-risk HPV detection; reflex LBC still requires clinician-collected cervical sample if HPV positive.
